text.title: Saga Pattern for Distributed Transactions Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for designing a saga.

rule.statement: Every step MUST have a paired compensation step that logically undoes it. A step without a compensation is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Partial saga failure with no compensation leaves the system in an inconsistent state — the very failure mode saga exists to prevent.

rule.statement: Every step AND every compensation MUST be idempotent. Redelivered events MUST produce the same end state.
rule.rationale: Message brokers redeliver; orchestrators retry. Non-idempotent steps double-charge, double-ship, double-decrement.

rule.statement: When a step both writes to local DB AND publishes an event, the write MUST go through a transactional outbox (or CDC). Direct dual-write (DB write + broker publish in code) is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Dual-write has a window where DB committed but publish failed — data is lost or duplicated.

rule.statement: On failure at step N, compensations MUST execute in reverse order: comp(N-1) → comp(N-2) → ... → comp(1). Forward-order or parallel compensation is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Compensations are not commutative — releasing inventory before refunding may surface to customer as 'item available' before refund completes.

rule.statement: Saga MUST declare one coordination style: orchestration (central orchestrator) OR choreography (services react to events). Mixing styles in one saga is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Hybrid coordination splits the saga state across two locations; debugging becomes impossible.
